title: Minimized window cannot be restored (frameless title bar)
description: Regression from nocx-cpp: after minimising, the window cannot be restored. Known Wails/macOS problem with hidden title bars — see wailsapp/wails#1319. TitleBarHiddenInset differs from TitleBarHidden only by UseToolbar:true and HideToolbarSeparator:true; the NSToolbar it adds is what insets the traffic lights and is the prime suspect for breaking deminiaturize. First try TitleBarHidden (traffic lights sit at the default position, so the tab strip's left padding shrinks accordingly). If that does not fix it, the fallback is returning to a normal title bar and reopening nocx-cpp with a different approach. Workaround meanwhile: Cmd+Tab, or right-click the Dock icon and Show.
acceptance_criteria: Minimising and restoring the window works, with the tab strip still serving as the title bar.
close_reason: TitleBarHiddenInset's NSToolbar (UseToolbar) was what broke deminiaturize; TitleBarHidden keeps the hidden title and full-size content and restores correctly. Confirmed by the user on the real window.

title: Single tab: clicking the window breaks input and corrupts the render
description: User repro: with exactly ONE tab open, click anywhere in the window. The prompt disappears, and typing then produces red garbled glyphs. Does not reproduce with two tabs. I reproduced a neighbouring symptom at :34115: after clicking the pane, typed characters never appear at all — the click does not hand focus to the terminal. Suspect the interaction between the click path, xterm focus, and either the refreshAtlas() call added for nocx-e27 or the --wails-draggable regions added with the frameless title bar. Debug in the browser at :34115, not by reading — this is invisible to jsdom.
acceptance_criteria: With one tab, clicking anywhere in the window keeps the prompt intact, focuses the terminal, and typing renders correctly.
close_reason: Verified by the repo owner in the real app: with one tab, clicking the window keeps the prompt and input works. Fixed by 25de485 (ResizeObserver repaints after clearing the texture atlas) and 2c02a46 (#panes sets --wails-draggable: no-drag, so Wails no longer swallows the mousedown). Guarded by e2e/click-focus.spec.ts for the shared click->focus->PTY path; see nocx-bq7 for what could not be guarded.

title: Tab switch corrupts the terminal: WebGL texture atlas lost when a pane is hidden
description: Switching away from a tab and back renders garbage — glyphs drawn from wrong atlas coordinates. Reproduced in Chrome via the wails dev server on :34115: fill a tab with block glyphs, switch away, switch back, and ████/▓▓▓▓ come back as a solid yellow rectangle and hatching, with a stray vertical bar across the screen. Text glyphs mostly survive; the damage is to the glyph atlas. Panes are hidden with visibility (see the lazy-mount comment in tabs.ts), which keeps the layout box but lets the WebGL renderer's texture atlas go stale. Likely fix: on activation, clear the atlas and force a full repaint — the WebGL addon exposes clearTextureAtlas(), and term.refresh(0, rows-1) redraws. Verify the fix by the same reproduction, not by unit tests: jsdom has no layout or GPU.
acceptance_criteria: Switching away from a filled tab and back renders identically to before the switch, verified by screenshot in the browser.
close_reason: Verified visually at :34115: filled a tab with block glyphs, switched away and back, and the render is identical to before the switch — no yellow blocks, no stray bars. Fixed via a refreshAtlas() seam on TerminalRenderer.

title: M0 terminal blank screen: ws:port emitted in OnStartup races frontend subscription
description: main.go startup() (OnStartup) emits ws:port via wailsRuntime.EventsEmit BEFORE the frontend subscribes (EventsOn in main.ts). Wails does not buffer events for late subscribers -> port promise hangs -> terminal.mount() never runs -> blank screen.
acceptance_criteria: Frontend gets the port via a bound GetWSPort() method (request/response), not a startup event. Add options.Debug{OpenInspectorOnStartup:true}. Terminal renders (app or localhost:34115).
close_reason: Closed

title: Composition root + slog logging (interface-first DI, observability)
description: AD-8 + observability. Constructor injection at one root; slog behind a logging interface.
close_reason: Composition root (internal/app) + slog logging interface (internal/log). Constructor injection wires all modules at one root. Tested.

title: ghostty-web <-> PTY: render output, send keystrokes, handle resize
description: Terminal core. PTY bytes -> ghostty-web write(); ghostty-web onData -> keystrokes to PTY; WebView resize -> {cols,rows,xpixel,ypixel} -> PTY (AD-1 resize). One tab, no tab bar.
acceptance_criteria: Typing runs commands in the local shell and output renders correctly; resizing the window reflows correctly.
close_reason: Closed

title: Minimal WS transport: bidirectional PTY byte stream for one session
description: Transport (AD-1 data plane, minimal). One WebSocket; stream PTY bytes as binary frames both ways for ONE session. Minimal control (open/resize); full JSON-RPC/AD-9/AD-10 are later stories.
acceptance_criteria: FE and BE exchange PTY bytes over WS for one session; keystrokes reach PTY; output reaches FE; resize propagates.
close_reason: Closed

title: PTY: spawn local shell + stream I/O
description: Terminal core (AD-6/AD-7). Spawn a local shell in a PTY (creack/pty); stream stdin/stdout; handle exit. Behind an interface (AD-8).
acceptance_criteria: Local shell runs in a PTY; bytes stream both ways; resize works; exit signaled; unit-tested with a fake.
close_reason: Closed

title: Quality gates: golangci-lint (strict) + gofumpt + pre-commit + GitHub Actions CI
description: Wire quality gates from commit #1 (AD/quality invariants): strict golangci-lint, gofumpt, pre-commit hooks (lint+format+test), GitHub Actions CI. No merge without green.
close_reason: Quality gates wired: .githooks/pre-commit (4 gates, verified red->green by the worker and re-verified independently by the coordinator), make hooks + make ci, .github/workflows/ci.yml (macos-latest backend, ubuntu frontend, golangci-lint pinned v1.64.8). First CI run still unverified — tracked separately.

title: Scaffold: Go module + Wails v2 app + ghostty-web frontend skeleton
description: Bootstrap the repo per docs/architecture.md module map: go.mod, Wails v2 app, ghostty-web frontend skeleton, composition root wiring.
close_reason: M0 scaffold: Go module + Wails v2 app + ghostty-web frontend skeleton. All backend packages (pty, ssh, session, transport, config, shellintegration) with interfaces + stubs; composition root wiring via DI; structured logging via slog adapter; quality gate configs; tests pass, lint clean.

title: Epic: Foundation
description: Repo scaffold + quality gates + DI/logging plumbing: Go module, Wails v2 app skeleton, xterm.js frontend skeleton, composition root (interface-first DI, AD-8), slog logging, golangci-lint/gofumpt/pre-commit/GitHub Actions CI.
close_reason: All three children complete: scaffold, composition root + slog, quality gates (hook + Makefile + GitHub Actions). First CI run still unverified — tracked in nocx-3xf.

title: Spike: verify ghostty-web OSC 7/133 + scrollback/selection/resize APIs and WASM perf
description: TOP architectural risk (docs/architecture.md AD-5/AD-6). Verify whether ghostty-web exposes OSC 7 (cwd) AND OSC 133 (prompt) handlers/events, plus scrollback/selection/resize APIs; measure WASM parser throughput with representative agent TUIs (Claude Code / aider) inside WKWebView. If OSC handlers are absent, evaluate patching ghostty-web vs backend-parses-OSC (which would break AD-6).
acceptance_criteria: Documented: does ghostty-web expose OSC 7/133 + scrollback/selection/resize? Perf numbers for a representative TUI. Recommendation recorded as an ADR in docs/decisions/. AD-5/AD-6 confirmed or revised.
close_reason: RESOLVED against ghostty-web. API surface: xterm.js exposes parser.registerOscHandler/registerCsiHandler/registerDcsHandler/registerEscHandler; ghostty-web exposes NO OSC handler registration at all — only OSC 8 hyperlinks, and its own types note the URI is 'not yet exposed in simplified API'. Verified empirically on xterm.js, not from docs: registered handlers for OSC 7 and OSC 133 and wrote real sequences — OSC 7 returned 'file://wemosd1/Users/shady/Documents/repos/nocx', OSC 133 returned all five markers A, B, C, D;0, D;127 including exit codes. So AD-5/AD-6 hold on xterm.js with no backend sniffing; on ghostty-web they would require patching the library.

title: [bash] OSC 133 D exit code always 0 — $? clobbered before capture
description: nocx.bash __nocx_prompt_command sets __nocx_in_prompt_command=1 (exit 0) before calling __nocx_precmd, which reads local __nocx_exit=$?. So D;<code> always reports 0 and command-failure detection is broken for local bash. Fix: capture $? as the first line of __nocx_prompt_command, pass to __nocx_precmd. Found in PR #1 review of nocx-5mn.4.

title: Raw-input routing after submit until next prompt
description: In RUNNING_RAW route all keys straight to the PTY until the next integrated prompt marker. No special interactive-stdin detection needed. Ctrl-C sends 0x03; alt-screen app owns input.
acceptance_criteria: read, python, node, less, vim, htop, password prompts, Ctrl-C, Ctrl-D all behave as a normal terminal while a command runs.

title: DOM input editor (textarea) + atomic submit handoff
description: Native <textarea> behind the editor (mouse caret, selection, multiline, IME, clipboard, native undo); avoid contenteditable. On submit: hide the editor BEFORE sending, then send the full text as one bracketed paste (ESC[200~ .. ESC[201~) + CR; let zle/readline paint the committed command into xterm once. Handoff, not echo suppression.
acceptance_criteria: Caret placeable anywhere by mouse; multiline + Unicode + IME + large paste work; on submit the command appears exactly once (committed in xterm), no duplicate prompt/echo.

title: InputTarget abstraction + registry; ShellInputTarget first
description: Editor is a passive surface; behavior comes from a registered InputTarget {id,label,submit,complete?,history?}. InputTargetRegistry.register/setActive/active. ShellInputTarget routes submit to the active PTY. Extensible: AgentInputTarget added later without touching the editor. Explicit mode switch (button+shortcut), never a magic prefix.
acceptance_criteria: A new capability is added by registering an InputTarget, not by editing the editor; ShellInputTarget submits to the PTY; active target is switchable explicitly.

title: Input-ownership state machine (RAW / PROMPT_READY / ALT_SCREEN)
description: Frontend state machine per ADR-0004. Transitions into enhanced states come ONLY from OSC 133 markers (A/B -> PROMPT_READY) and xterm alt-buffer event (-> ALT_SCREEN); after submit -> RUNNING_RAW until next prompt. Do NOT infer 'process reading stdin' from bytes/termios. Fail-open: missing/malformed markers, nested SSH, PS2 continuation -> fall back to conventional terminal; never trap the user in the DOM editor.
acceptance_criteria: State is one of RAW/PROMPT_READY/ALT_SCREEN; OSC 133 A/B enters PROMPT_READY; submit enters RUNNING_RAW; onBufferChange('alternate') enters ALT_SCREEN; absent markers keep a working plain terminal.

title: Epic: Command input & blocks (input-ownership state machine)
description: Warp-style command experience per ADR-0004. The command being composed is freely-editable DOM text (a real editor), decoupled from PTY line discipline; the same surface later hosts LLM agent input. Two orthogonal axes: (1) an input-ownership state machine (RAW / PROMPT_READY / ALT_SCREEN) where nocx owns input ONLY at a positively-identified integrated shell prompt and everything else routes raw to the PTY; (2) the editor as a passive surface behind a pluggable InputTarget registry (ShellInputTarget first, AgentInputTarget later). Prompt/echo handled by marker-only prompt + atomic handoff (hide editor -> bracketed-paste + CR), no stty / no readline mirroring. Output stays in one continuous xterm with OSC-133 decorations; freeze-to-DOM is deferred. See docs/decisions/0004-input-ownership-and-editor-abstraction.md.

title: OSC 52: programs cannot reach the clipboard — xterm.js drops the sequence
description:
xterm.js registers OSC handlers for 0,1,2,4,8,10,11,12,104,110,111,112 and silently discards everything else, so OSC 52 never arrives. Any program that copies on the user's behalf therefore reaches nothing, with no error: Claude Code (verified in binary 2.1.217 — ']52;c;' with a tmux branch, plus pbcopy/xclip/xsel/wl-copy fallbacks), tmux, vim with clipboard=unnamed, and everything over SSH where OSC 52 is the only route to a local clipboard at all. This is a silent regression against every terminal the user already runs.

Fix through parser.registerOscHandler(52, ...) — the same mechanism already carrying OSC 7 in frontend/src/renderers/xterm.ts:188. registerOscHandler accepts arbitrary identifiers, so no engine patch is needed.

Do NOT copy Tabby's approach (tabby-terminal/src/middleware/oscProcessing.ts): it re-parses the byte stream ahead of the engine, which duplicates VT parsing that AD-6 assigns to the VT frontend, and at line 57 it buffers the remainder without bound or timeout when no terminator arrives — a stream carrying an unterminated sequence stalls output while accumulating memory. Registering on the engine's parser inherits chunk reassembly and both terminator forms for free; the engine also already caps an OSC payload at 1e7 chars.

Write only. The read form (]52;c;?) must have no code path, so a remote host on an SSH session cannot exfiltrate the clipboard. iTerm2, kitty and wezterm take the same stance; Tabby arrives there implicitly by honouring only targets 'c' and empty.
acceptance_criteria: printf '\033]52;c;<base64>\007' places the decoded text on the system clipboard; selecting text inside a Claude Code session running in nocx reaches the clipboard; the read form is ignored entirely.
close_reason: OSC 52 arrives through parser.registerOscHandler(52,...) beside the existing OSC 7 handler — no byte-stream middleware, so no repeat of Tabby's unbounded buffer. Writes are denied by default with a Warp-style banner carrying the remedy (allow / don't show again / dismiss); the read form has no code path at all. Decoder rejects the read form, non-clipboard targets, malformed base64, zero-byte payloads, and oversized ones before decoding. Verified by hand: printf of an OSC 52 sequence leaves the clipboard untouched after dismissing the banner. Note a local program can still reach the clipboard via pbcopy — no terminal gates that, Warp included; this protects what has no second door, i.e. content over SSH.

title: [bug] Glyph corruption on resize and after closing a tab
description:
Two reproductions of what is almost certainly one renderer defect, both reported with screenshots by the owner.
(a) Resize the window: existing glyphs turn into mangled overlapping shapes.
(b) Close the last tab, land on the neighbour, type: red garbage appears instead of characters.
25de485 made the ResizeObserver call refreshAtlas() instead of a bare clearTextureAtlas(), which was supposed to cover (a); it is evidently not enough, or the repaint happens before the new grid is in place. Note the tab-activation path also calls refreshAtlas(), which is the (b) path.
An earlier e2e attempt at this failed to discriminate because it nudged the pane by 1px on purpose to avoid a reflow — the real trigger changes the grid. A test driving page.setViewportSize() to a genuinely different cols/rows should reproduce it (see nocx-bq7).
acceptance_criteria: A real window resize and a close-tab-then-type both leave the screen rendering correctly, and an e2e test fails without the fix.
notes:
e2e pixel-test investigation (worker task_42e778c10d9f), 2026-07-22:

Engines tried:
- Playwright Chromium (headless): WebGL IS active (confirmed via canvas webgl2 context probe of the xterm renderer canvas). Pixel tests pass identically with the fix reverted — the corruption does NOT reproduce in headless Chromium. This was already known from a prior attempt.
- Playwright WebKit (headless): WebGL IS active (same confirmation method). Pixel tests ALSO pass identically with the fix reverted — the corruption does NOT reproduce in headless WebKit either.

Conclusion: no automated guard is achievable from this harness. The glyph corruption (mangled overlapping glyphs on resize, red garbage after close-tab-and-type) is specific to the real macOS WKWebView rendering context inside the Wails app. Neither Playwright engine reproduces it.

xterm.ts change (IN PLACE and UNVERIFIED against visible symptom as of handoff):
refreshAtlas() formerly called clearTextureAtlas() then term.refresh(0, rows-1). It now calls only term.refresh(0, rows-1). Rationale: clearing the texture atlas before repainting races with atlas repopulation in _updateModel. The resize path (safeFit to fit to handleResize) already refreshes the char atlas via _refreshCharAtlas(). The tab-activation path needs only a viewport refresh.

Steps 4-5 (real-app verification) are PENDING a human check:
- Resize the real WKWebView window: check for mangled overlapping glyphs
- Close a tab and type on the survivor: check for red garbage
- One tab, type: check prompt stays intact (nocx-d1f regression)

Cleanup done:
- e2e/screenshots/ deleted (leftover PNGs from prior investigation)
- /e2e/screenshots/ added to .gitignore
- No pixel test shipped (would pass in both states = false guard)

If the fix is wrong: the refreshAtlas change is the only change. To revert, restore clearTextureAtlas() calls before term.refresh().
close_reason:
Verified by the repo owner in the real WKWebView app, which is the only place this reproduces: resize no longer mangles glyphs, close-tab-then-type shows no red garbage, and nocx-d1f has not regressed (one tab, click, type renders correctly) — that last check mattered because the fix removes the clearTextureAtlas() call that 25de485 had paired with a refresh.

Fix: refreshAtlas() in frontend/src/renderers/xterm.ts now only calls term.refresh(0, rows-1); the clearTextureAtlas() call is gone, because clearing before repainting races with atlas repopulation in _updateModel while the resize path already refreshes the char atlas via _refreshCharAtlas().

No automated guard ships, deliberately. Both Playwright engines were tried with the fix reverted — Chromium and WebKit, WebGL confirmed active in each — and NEITHER reproduces the corruption. A test that passes in both states is a false guard, so none was written; the finding is recorded in the notes instead. Our e2e harness is structurally blind to this class of bug.

title: Playwright webServer leaves wails dev and its vite watcher running
description: Driving 'wails dev' from Playwright's webServer does not tear down cleanly, so a run hangs for minutes after the last test and would burn a CI job's timeout. Measured: the frontend dev watcher runs in its OWN process group (vite PGID 33952, parent 'npm run dev' reparented to PPID 1) while Playwright's group is separate (33771), so neither Playwright's group kill nor a 'trap kill 0' in the webServer command can reach it. The orphan inherits the run's stdio, so with stdout/stderr piped the stream never reaches EOF; one observed run only exited after vite happened to crash with EPIPE, 3.4 minutes for 7 seconds of tests. Runs also leave 'wails dev' itself alive afterwards. Options: a globalTeardown that reaps by port and by the project-scoped vite path, or a globalSetup that starts the server under setsid and kills the whole tree.
acceptance_criteria: A full e2e run exits within seconds of the last test and leaves no wails dev, app or vite process behind.
close_reason: Fixed by gracefulShutdown: {signal: 'SIGTERM'} in playwright.config.ts. Root cause confirmed in the wails v2.13.0 source: the CLI starts the frontend watcher with Setpgid (dev_other.go:15) and reaps it from a SIGTERM handler via syscall.Kill(-pgid) (dev_other.go:32, dev.go:90/114). Playwright's default is to SIGKILL the group, so that handler never ran. Verified from a clean process table: 4 passed in 15.7s, 16.4s wall clock (was 3m26s for fewer tests), and no wails dev, app or vite process left behind.

title: e2e regression test for nocx-d1f: click-to-focus and atlas repaint
description: nocx-d1f was fixed in 25de485 (ResizeObserver refreshAtlas) and 2c02a46 (#panes --wails-draggable: no-drag) but no test guards either half, so both can silently regress. The bug class is invisible to jsdom: no layout, no GPU, no focus. Add an e2e spec on the existing Playwright harness at :34115 covering (a) a click on the pane hands focus to the terminal and typed characters reach it, (b) the pane render is stable across the deferred ResizeObserver run rather than being left on a cleared atlas.
acceptance_criteria: A spec in e2e/ passes against the current tree and fails when the 25de485 fix is reverted.
notes:
Landed: e2e/click-focus.spec.ts (click -> focus -> keystroke -> PTY -> tab title round trip), plus a self-starting webServer in playwright.config.ts.
Deliberately NOT landed: an e2e guard for the atlas half. An attempt (1px sub-cell nudge to fire the ResizeObserver without reflowing the grid, then a forced row repaint, compared against a tab-switch refreshAtlas as ground truth) passed identically with 25de485 reverted — verified the revert really reached the browser by curling the served module (clearTextureAtlas twice, no refreshAtlas). xterm re-rasterizes glyphs lazily on the next draw, so a cleared atlas repaints correctly from outside; the original corruption needed a tighter race than a test can stage. A vacuous test is worse than none, so it was deleted.
Still open as a follow-up: guard the invariant itself (an atlas clear is always followed by a refresh) at unit level, driving a stubbed ResizeObserver in the vitest suite — jsdom has no WebGL, but term.refresh() is still observable.

title: Terminal leaked its launcher's agent session into every shell
description: nocx handed the whole parent environment to every PTY (cmd.Env = os.Environ()), so when it is started from inside a coding agent — which is how it gets developed — each shell inherited that agent's session markers. A 'claude' run in a nocx tab saw CLAUDE_CODE_CHILD_SESSION and silently turned transcript saving off; the user hit this for real. The principle is general: a terminal hands out shells and must not hand out its launcher's identity with them. Fixed by scrubbing a precise list of session markers (not a CLAUDE* wildcard — stripping something like an API key would break the very tool being fixed). Related and still open: nocx does not start a LOGIN shell, so the environment is whatever nocx inherited rather than what the user's profile defines. Terminal.app runs the first shell with -l. Worth a separate look.
acceptance_criteria: A shell opened in nocx carries no launcher session markers; credentials and ordinary variables survive.
close_reason: Closed

title: Tier A shell hooks: make the shell emit OSC 133 command markers
description:
nocx-5mn.1 verified we can RECEIVE OSC 133 (ADR-0001: xterm.js returned A, B, C, D;0 and D;127 with exit codes). Nothing yet makes a shell SEND them, so every consumer of command boundaries is starved. AD-5 Tier A is exactly this: shell hooks with zero remote install, working locally and over SSH. Ship the zsh/bash hooks and inject them so a user gets this without configuring anything. Emit A at prompt start, B at prompt end, C when output starts, and D with the exit code when the command finishes.

SCOPE CORRECTION (2026-07-22): the old description claimed this unlocks the activity indicator 'driven by real command completion instead of byte heuristics (nocx-5mf)'. That is dead. nocx-5mf shipped in 2d990ee via the title-status layer (frontend/src/agent-status.ts + Tab.updateAgentStatus) and its close reason records the OSC 133 dependency as a modelling error: the two are complementary, since OSC 133 marks SHELL command boundaries and cannot see inside one long-running agent command. Do not cite nocx-5mf as justification here again — it cost a session of picking already-done work.

What genuinely remains behind these markers, and nothing else: command blocks, success/failure colouring from the D exit code, and 'a background command finished' as an explicit event rather than a byte heuristic.
acceptance_criteria: Opening a shell in nocx produces OSC 133 A/B/C/D at the right boundaries, D carries the exit code, and the frontend receives them through the registered handler.
notes: SCOPE ADDITION (2026-07-23, ADR-0004): the injected hooks must ALSO install a marker-only, visually empty prompt in enhanced mode (empty PROMPT/PS1 carrying only the OSC markers), saving and restoring the user's original prompt when integration is off or nocx exits. This is what lets the DOM input editor (nocx-4ff) own composition without a duplicate shell prompt. Raw fallback stays mandatory: if hooks/markers are absent, behave as a conventional terminal.

title: Activity indicator is always on for full-screen TUIs
description: Tabs running Claude Code or Hermes show the activity indicator permanently: those TUIs animate spinners and elapsed-time counters, so bytes never stop arriving, and any output currently counts as activity. The indicator answers 'did bytes arrive' when the user needs 'did something happen worth my attention'. Note Tabby has the identical flaw — baseTerminalTab.component.ts subscribes binaryOutput$ straight to displayActivity() — so copying it faithfully is what produced this; do not look there for the fix. Proposed rule: in the normal screen buffer any output is activity, as today; in the ALTERNATE screen buffer repaints are ignored, because a full-screen TUI redrawing itself is not news — but a bell (BEL) always counts, since that is a program explicitly asking for attention. xterm.js exposes buffer.active.type and onBell. Keep AD-6 intact: the renderer reports the facts (which buffer is active, a bell fired), the tab decides the policy — tabs.ts must not sniff bytes.
design:
How Warp does it: it does not guess from output — the SHELL tells it, via OSC 133 semantic prompt markers. A = prompt start, B = prompt end / input start, C = command output start, D;<exit> = command finished with its exit code. Ghostty, iTerm2, Kitty and WezTerm use the same substrate. 'A command finished in a background tab' is therefore an explicit event, not a heuristic, and it carries success or failure with it.

We already proved we can receive this. ADR-0001 chose xterm.js precisely because registering parser.registerOscHandler returned all five OSC 133 markers — A, B, C, D;0 and D;127 — exit codes included, where ghostty-web exposed no OSC handler registration at all. AD-5 already names OSC 133 as the Tier A substrate and epic nocx-5mn owns the shell hooks.

So the indicator should be driven by OSC 133 D, not by counting bytes: light it when a command finishes in a background tab, and it can then say more than 'something happened' — a failed command (D;non-zero) deserves a different colour from a successful one, which is what Warp shows.

Scope boundary worth stating: OSC 133 marks SHELL command boundaries. It fires when a long build ends, and when claude itself exits — but it says nothing while claude is running and wants attention mid-session, because that is inside one shell command. That case needs the program to signal: a bell, or OSC 9. Claude Code currently emits neither (see anthropics/claude-code issues #22528 and #32635, both open requests for OSC 133 support). Keep the bell rule for it and do not pretend the byte heuristic can cover it.

This makes nocx-5mf depend on the Tier A shell hooks in nocx-5mn rather than on more heuristics.
acceptance_criteria: A tab running a spinner TUI in the alternate screen stops showing a permanent activity indicator; a bell inside that TUI still lights it; ordinary shell output in the normal buffer still lights it.
notes:
How Orca does the spinning tab indicator (/Users/shady/Documents/repos/orca, src/shared/terminal-title-status.ts): detectAgentStatusFromTitle(title) classifies the TERMINAL TITLE into three states — 'working', 'idle', 'permission' — not two. Markers: Claude Code prefixes the title with ✳ when idle and with a braille spinner while working; Gemini CLI has its own symbols for all three; Pi/OMP use synthetic labels; a braille spinner anywhere in the title means working. Cursor's bare native title is explicitly excluded as carrying no status.

We can do this today with what we already have: renderer.onTitle is wired, and the user's own screenshot showed a nocx tab titled '✳ Claude Code' — the marker was arriving and we discarded it. No new protocol, no shell hooks, nothing to wait for.

'permission' is the state worth the loudest treatment: the agent is blocked on a question, which is strictly more urgent than 'finished'.

Two design lessons worth copying from Orca's comments, both learned the hard way there:
- It keeps the title layer and the agent-hook layer as SEPARATE evidence, because consumers combine them differently, and a single merged status silently changes behaviour.
- 'Evidence that something runs is not proof of who': it splits activity classification from agent identity, since a bare spinner says something is working but not what. Do not collapse the two.

Relationship to nocx-5mn.4 (OSC 133): complementary, not competing. OSC 133 D marks SHELL command boundaries — the build finished, claude exited. Title status covers what happens INSIDE one long-running command, which is exactly the Claude Code case OSC 133 cannot see. Build both; they answer different questions.
close_reason: Solved by the title-status layer: a braille spinner means working, Claude Code's ✳ means waiting, and a waiting background tab lights the underline. The OSC 133 dependency was my own modelling error — those markers are complementary (they cover shell command boundaries, which cannot see inside a long-running agent) and remain as nocx-5mn.4, not a blocker for this.

title: Tab title goes blank when a TUI exits (empty OSC 0/2 is taken literally)
description: Run claude code in a tab and exit it: the tab label becomes empty. On exit the program emits an OSC 0/2 with an empty string to clear the title, and Tab.updateTitle writes it straight into textContent, so the tab loses its name and cannot be identified. An empty or whitespace-only title must be ignored and the tab must fall back to its default name.
acceptance_criteria: After a TUI that clears the title exits, the tab shows its fallback name rather than nothing.
close_reason: Closed

title: Frameless window: tabs live in the title bar (Tabby-style)
description: Tabby has no separate title bar: the tab strip IS the title bar, with the macOS traffic lights inset to the left of the first tab. nocx currently renders the native Wails title bar (Title: "nocx" in main.go) with the tab strip as a second row below it, which is the biggest structural difference from Tabby and wastes ~28px of vertical space. Needs Wails Frameless plus the macOS titlebar options (TitleBarHiddenInset / traffic-light inset), a -webkit-app-region drag area on the empty part of the tab strip, and left padding so the tabs clear the traffic lights. Overlaps the app-shell work in nocx-8yg.9 — do them together or sequence deliberately.
acceptance_criteria: The window has no separate native title bar; tabs sit in the title bar area with the traffic lights inset to their left; dragging the empty part of the strip moves the window.
close_reason: Landed in 2c02a46 (commit body already said 'Closes nocx-cpp' but the bead was never closed). Frameless via mac.TitleBarHidden; .tabbar is the title bar with 78px left padding and --wails-draggable: drag, children set back to no-drag. Deviation from the acceptance wording: traffic lights are NOT inset — TitleBarHiddenInset's NSToolbar broke deminiaturize (nocx-dqg, P0), so TitleBarHidden was chosen and the padding clears the lights at their default position.

title: Spine: name the frontend as a first-class quality surface in architecture.md
description: docs/architecture.md:172 Testing strategy enumerates Go surfaces only — 'unit tests per module against interfaces with injected fakes; integration tests across the transport boundary and the session/PTY path'. The module map describes frontend modules (terminal, ui, ipc) with their SRP, but the testing strategy never names them, so a literal reader can conclude the frontend is not a test surface. That gap is how a P0 defect (ipc.ts connect() never resolving) reached review with every gate green. Rewrite the Testing strategy and Quality gates paragraphs to cover the frontend explicitly: tests, lint and formatting on equal footing with Go. AGENTS.md requires changing the spine deliberately rather than routing around it.
acceptance_criteria: architecture.md states frontend tests/lint/format as mandatory; AGENTS.md and README agree with it.
close_reason: Control plane + session registry landed and reviewed over four rounds. JSON-RPC 2.0 open/close/resize + exit notification over text frames; binary data plane framed as version|msgType|16-byte session-id|payload with the metadata type reserved; server-authoritative ids from crypto/rand; PTY created at open's size (proven end-to-end via stty size); real session.Reg behind an injected PTYFactory (AD-8), concrete wiring at the composition root only. Spine updated: frontend is a peer quality surface, CI claims corrected to release-only. 64 tests passing, all gates green.

title: Wire frontend test/lint/format into all three enforcement points
description: The pre-commit hook, make ci and .github/workflows/ci.yml each run only npx tsc --noEmit (plus npm run build) for the frontend. Once the test harness (nocx-tqf) and eslint/prettier (see sibling) exist, add them to all three so the frontend is gated exactly like Go. Per the user's decision the hook must FAIL, not SKIP, when frontend/node_modules is absent — the same way it already fails on a missing gofumpt; the SKIP was a concession to the frontend being second-class. Update the README Quality gates matrix, which currently has no test/lint/format rows for the frontend at all.
acceptance_criteria: Hook, make ci and CI each run frontend test + lint + format:check; README matrix matches reality; hook fails with an actionable message when node_modules is missing.
close_reason: Frontend gated exactly like Go: eslint+prettier+vitest wired into pre-commit, make ci and CI; 39 frontend tests; connect() regression mutation-verified; golden wire vector pinned on both sides.

title: Frontend linter + formatter on equal footing with Go
description: Go has golangci-lint (strict) and gofumpt enforced from every commit; the frontend has neither. tsc --noEmit checks types only — it is the frontend's go vet, not its linter, and not a formatter at all. Add eslint (with typescript-eslint) and prettier to frontend/, configured to match the existing code style rather than reflowing the whole tree, so a diff stays reviewable. Both must be runnable as npm scripts.
acceptance_criteria: npm run lint and npm run format:check exist in frontend/package.json, both pass on the current tree, and a deliberately mis-formatted file fails format:check.
close_reason: Frontend gated exactly like Go: eslint+prettier+vitest wired into pre-commit, make ci and CI; 39 frontend tests; connect() regression mutation-verified; golden wire vector pinned on both sides.

title: Frontend has no test harness — a P0 defect shipped past every gate
description: During nocx-2ho.2 review the coordinator found that ipc.ts connect() returned a promise that never resolved, so tabs.ts hung on await and no terminal could ever appear. gofumpt, golangci-lint, go test -race, tsc --noEmit and npm run build were all green — none of them can execute frontend code. The Go side has 30 tests; the frontend has zero. Any behavioural regression in ipc.ts/tabs.ts/renderers reaches the user. Needs a runtime test harness (vitest is the default for a Vite project) covering at minimum: the connect->open->sessionId lifecycle, frame encode/decode against the Go wire contract in internal/transport/frame.go, and the streaming-TextDecoder split-UTF-8 case from nocx-ao7.
acceptance_criteria: A frontend test command exists, runs in the pre-commit hook and in CI, and a test fails if connect() stops resolving.
close_reason: Frontend gated exactly like Go: eslint+prettier+vitest wired into pre-commit, make ci and CI; 39 frontend tests; connect() regression mutation-verified; golden wire vector pinned on both sides.

title: Drop the ghostty-web dependency; rewrite README for contributors
description: User decision 2026-07-21: ghostty-web is not part of the project. Removed the library (renderers/ghostty.ts, console-filter.ts which existed solely as its log-spam workaround, package.json/lock) and scrubbed it from AGENTS.md, docs/architecture.md and docs/vision.md. ADR-0001 deliberately keeps its ghostty-web content — it is the dated record of why xterm.js won. Competitor 'Ghostty' positioning in vision.md/README kept: that is product framing, not a technology choice. TerminalRenderer + wterm kept so the VT engine stays swappable. README rewritten from stale 'early planning' prose into a working contributor guide: prerequisites, make hooks first, quality gates, beads workflow, sources of truth, layout, verified licenses.
close_reason: Done and verified by the coordinator: zero ghostty-web library references outside docs/decisions/, all grep survivors are competitor positioning; ADR-0001 untouched; TerminalRenderer + wterm intact; pre-commit hook green (gofumpt, golangci-lint, go test -race, tsc); npm run build succeeds. Not committed — conservative profile.

title: Verify the first GitHub Actions run is actually green
description: CI was authored and passes actionlint, but has never executed — no push has happened. Unverified pairings that can only fail on a real runner: golangci-lint-action@v4 driving golangci-lint v1.64.8; setup-go resolving go-version 1.26 on macos-latest; npm ci + tsc + vite build on the ubuntu frontend job; whether go test -race ./... (internal/pty spawns real shells via creack/pty) is stable on a GitHub macOS runner. Watch the first run and fix whatever breaks.
acceptance_criteria: First Actions run on main is green, or the failures are fixed and a subsequent run is green.
close_reason: Run 29822238195 (workflow_dispatch on main) green: backend 2m30s — gofumpt, golangci-lint v1.64.8, go build, go test -race all pass; frontend npm ci + tsc + vite build pass. Root cause of the first failure was golangci-lint-action@v4 shipping a Go-1.24-built binary against go.mod's go 1.26; fixed in 0adab78 by installing the linter with go install under setup-go's Go 1.26.

title: [bake-off] ghostty-web: seams between cells on Retina — canvas-2d fills fractional cell rects, no font size avoids it
description: Big block glyphs render with a visible grid of seams in the ghostty-web tab; xterm.js draws the same art solid. Cause: ghostty-web does ctx.scale(devicePixelRatio) then fillRect(x, y, metrics.width, metrics.height) in CSS pixels, so every cell edge lands between physical pixels and gets antialiased. Reproduced in isolation without WASM: adjacent fillRects of 8.4 CSS px at DPR 2 show seams, 9.0 CSS px are solid. Not fixable by configuration — the font stack's advance/fontSize ratio is 0.6021, so no integer font size from 12 to 20 yields an integer device-pixel cell width. There is also no WebGL path to switch to: the library contains zero webgl/gpu references, canvas-2d only. Same root class as nocx-4wk (blur from imageSmoothing/DPR). Fixing it would mean patching ghostty-web to snap cell metrics to whole device pixels.
notes: CORRECTION: 'not fixable by configuration' was wrong — only integer font sizes were tested. The advance is exactly linear (0.60205em/cell), so fractional sizes do land the cell on whole device pixels: 14.12 -> 17.002 device px, 16.61 -> 20.000 device px at DPR 2. FONT_SIZE set to 14.12 to test. Still unverified: cell HEIGHT (horizontal seams) is derived from font metrics/LINE_HEIGHT and was not measured, and this uses measureText('M') which may differ from ghostty-web's internal metric. Also note the deeper option: ghostty-web's WASM core exposes a full renderer-facing API (getLine/getViewport/isRowDirty/markClean/needsFullRedraw/getCursor/getColors), so a custom WebGL renderer on top of the ghostty VT core is possible — it just means writing from scratch what xterm.js's WebglAddon already provides.
close_reason: ghostty-web dropped from the project (user decision 2026-07-21). These three bugs are ghostty-web-specific rendering defects (imageSmoothing/DPR blur, Retina cell seams from fractional canvas-2d rects, scroll reset on new output) and no longer describe any code we ship. xterm.js is the VT frontend per ADR-0001.

title: [bake-off] ghostty-web resets scroll position to bottom on new output
description: Observed in hermes: scrolling up in the ghostty-web tab is undone by the next chunk of output, so the view is constantly thrown back to the bottom. xterm.js holds the user's scroll position under the same load. For an agent terminal whose status line ticks every second this makes scrollback effectively unusable. Not reproduced in an automated harness yet — headless virtual-time racing the WASM load made the measurement flaky — so this rests on direct observation. Renderer-specific, so it is a genuine bake-off differentiator against ghostty-web.
close_reason: ghostty-web dropped from the project (user decision 2026-07-21). These three bugs are ghostty-web-specific rendering defects (imageSmoothing/DPR blur, Retina cell seams from fractional canvas-2d rects, scroll reset on new output) and no longer describe any code we ship. xterm.js is the VT frontend per ADR-0001.

title: [bug] Resize forwarded to PTY on every intermediate grid size — SIGWINCH storm shreds TUIs
description: tabs.ts forwarded every renderer onResize straight to ws.sendResize with no debounce and no dedupe, so dragging a window edge walks the PTY through every intermediate grid size. Each one is a SIGWINCH and a full TUI repaint for a size that is already stale — visible as the screen falling apart during resize, identically in all three renderers (shared code, not the engines). Fix: skip unchanged cols/rows and debounce the PTY notification by RESIZE_SETTLE_MS (80ms); local fitting stays immediate.
acceptance_criteria: Dragging the window edge produces one PTY resize on settle, not one per intermediate size; TUI redraws cleanly
close_reason: Fixed in frontend/src/tabs.ts: dedupe unchanged grid sizes + 80ms trailing debounce before notifying the PTY.

title: [bug] WS frames decoded with a fresh TextDecoder — split UTF-8 sequences become U+FFFD
description: ipc.ts decoded every WebSocket frame with 'new TextDecoder().decode(buf)'. A PTY read ends at an arbitrary byte offset, so multi-byte UTF-8 routinely straddles two frames; a per-frame decoder severs the sequence and emits U+FFFD for each half. U+FFFD renders as a question mark in a diamond, identically in every renderer, so it reads as a font/glyph bug and sent the renderer bake-off chasing ghosts. Reproduced live against the real PTY+WS: braille art through a 40-col PTY gave 10x U+FFFD with the old path, 0 with a single streaming decoder. Fix: one TextDecoder per connection, decode(buf, {stream:true}).
acceptance_criteria: Multi-byte UTF-8 split across WS frames renders intact; verified end-to-end against a live PTY
close_reason: Fixed in frontend/src/ipc.ts: one TextDecoder per connection with {stream:true}. Verified end-to-end against a live PTY — 10x U+FFFD before, 0 after.

title: [bug] PTY inherits no locale when app is launched from Finder → TUIs downgrade UTF-8 to '?'
description: pty_local.go sets only TERM: cmd.Env = append(os.Environ(), "TERM=xterm-256color"). A macOS .app launched from Finder/Dock has no LANG/LC_* in its environment, so the child shell has no locale. Python/Rich/prompt_toolkit TUIs then compute a non-UTF-8 stdout encoding and encode with errors='replace', which emits literal '?' bytes for every non-ASCII glyph — identical in every renderer, so it masquerades as a font bug. Launching via 'wails dev' from a terminal hides this because LANG is inherited. Fix: default LANG/LC_CTYPE to a UTF-8 locale in NewLocal when absent.
acceptance_criteria: Shell spawned by a Finder-launched nocx.app reports a UTF-8 locale; non-ASCII TUI output renders without '?' substitution
close_reason: Fixed in internal/pty/pty_local.go: withUTF8Locale() defaults LANG=en_US.UTF-8 when the inherited environment states no locale at all (LC_ALL/LC_CTYPE/LANG all absent), which is the Finder/Dock launch case. An inherited locale is never overridden, including a deliberate LANG=C. Table-driven test covers all five cases; go test -race, gofumpt and golangci-lint all green.

title: Renderer bake-off: tabbed A/B harness (ghostty-web vs xterm.js vs wterm)
description: Cannot pick a VT frontend by staring at one at a time. Put each renderer behind its own tab, each with its own PTY session (backend already spawns one PTY per WS connection), so the same workload can be run in each and compared side by side. Feeds the renderer decision that nocx-dej (ghostty-web API spike) and nocx-4wk (render bugs) both hang on.
acceptance_criteria: Tab bar switches between ghostty-web / xterm.js / wterm; each tab has a live independent shell; switching preserves session state; Cmd+1/2/3 shortcuts; status shows engine + grid size
close_reason: Bake-off harness delivered its purpose — three renderers side by side, each with its own live PTY, drove the renderer decision. Tabs stay in the codebase for re-testing future candidates.

title: M0 render (data-backed): blur=imageSmoothing reset on resize; top-clip=canvas 6px taller than container; scroll needs instrumentation
description: Coordinator inspected the live page via JS. BLUR: HiDPI is correct (backing 3672x1860=CSS 1836x930 x dpr2, ctx scale x2) but ctx.imageSmoothingEnabled=true; canvas.width/height on resize resets context state -> imageSmoothing reverts to true -> blur. TOP-CLIP: canvas CSS height 930 is 6px taller than #terminal container 924 (overflow:hidden), and 17px narrower -> non-integer rows -> partial row. SCROLL: terminal instance not exposed; needs instrumentation.
acceptance_criteria: Blur gone (imageSmoothing=false enforced after resize); canvas fits container (no clip); window.__term exposed + wheel/write logs viewportY & alt-screen for scroll diagnosis.
close_reason: ghostty-web dropped from the project (user decision 2026-07-21). These three bugs are ghostty-web-specific rendering defects (imageSmoothing/DPR blur, Retina cell seams from fractional canvas-2d rects, scroll reset on new output) and no longer describe any code we ship. xterm.js is the VT frontend per ADR-0001.

title: M0 agent-TUI render: blurry after fit() (DPR) + alt-screen scroll snaps to bottom
description: Two frontend/ghostty-web issues (plain terminal is fine). (1) Text became blurry after the scroll fix added terminal.fit() in main.ts -> canvas likely no longer sized for devicePixelRatio on resize, or fit() runs before layout. (2) In agent TUIs (alternate screen), scrolling up snaps back to bottom because ghostty-web auto-scrolls the viewport to bottom on every write and agents redraw constantly.
acceptance_criteria: Text is crisp again (Retina), initial PTY size still synced (no overflow); in an agent TUI scrolling up no longer snaps to bottom.
close_reason: Closed

title: Agent TUIs scroll: PTY winsize never synced to rendered grid (stuck 80x24)
description: ws.go handleSession spawns the PTY hardcoded at Cols:80 Rows:24; frontend/src/main.ts never sends the initial fitted size to the PTY after connect (only wires terminal.onResize for future resizes). PTY winsize stays 80x24 while ghostty-web renders the real window size -> agent TUIs read the wrong winsize via ioctl -> full-screen redraw overflows -> constant downward scroll.
acceptance_criteria: Frontend sends the initial size to the PTY right after connect (fit + sendResize). Agent TUI / top fills the correct area, no constant scroll; window resize reflows.
close_reason: Closed

title: App shell: collapsible left sidebar (VS Code-style) — home for SSH host list / sessions

title: Session multiplexing over one WS (multi-session)
description: AD-1. Route binary frames by 16-byte session-id; concurrent sessions.
close_reason: One WebSocket, N sessions: WSClient is multi-session with a per-session streaming TextDecoder; tabs share one client. Backend already multiplexed — proven by TestWSServer_TwoSessionsOneConnection_Isolation rather than rewritten.

title: Backpressure: per-session in-flight-byte credit; throttle source
description: AD-10. Bounded credit; throttle PTY/SSH read when full; lossless+ordered; per-session fairness.
close_reason: AD-10 credit flow control: per-session 64KB in-flight window derived from ring state, FairChunk yield so a flooding tab cannot monopolise the shared writer. Fixed a stall bug where the window waited for a fully-acked stream — a condition the client can never meet.

title: Reconnect: bounded per-session output ring keyed by byte-offset; replay/reset
description: AD-9. Backend replay ring; FE acks offset; replay-from-offset or reset. Transport buffer, not scrollback.
notes: ADR-0002: the alternative design — server holds a VT grid and repaints the client on reattach, as zellij does — is closed to us by AD-6, not merely unchosen. Do not re-propose it as a shortcut. The ring stays a transport buffer keyed by byte-offset.
close_reason: Reconnect end to end: backend replay ring keyed by byte-offset with attach/ack, sessions survive a dropped WS; frontend tracks per-session offsets, throttled acks, reconnects with backoff and reattaches, handling resumed vs reset (full renderer reset + fresh decoder).

JSON-RPC 2.0 open/close/resize + exit notification over text frames; binary data plane framed as version|msgType|16-byte session-id|payload with the metadata type reserved; server-authoritative ids from crypto/rand; PTY created at open's size (proven end-to-end via stty size); real session.Reg behind an injected PTYFactory (AD-8), concrete wiring at the composition root only.
