title: Epic: Terminal core
description: ghostty-web integration + rendering; pty module (local shells); session model — registry, one PTY/channel + goroutine per tab (AD-6/AD-7).

title: writeFileAtomic replaces a symlinked ~/.zshrc with a private copy
description: shellintegration.go writeFileAtomic does os.Rename(tmp, rcPath); if ~/.zshrc/.bashrc is a symlink into a dotfiles repo (chezmoi/stow/yadm), the rename replaces the symlink with a standalone 0600 file — breaks the link and drops the user's perms. Detect symlinks and write through to the resolved target (or append in place). Review finding #6, PR #1 / nocx-5mn.4.

title: [bash] gate line aborts rc under set -u; changing it needs rc migration
description: The rc gate '[[ -n "$NOCX_SHELL_INTEGRATION" ]] && source ...' errors under set -u in non-nocx shells (var unbound). Fix needs ${NOCX_SHELL_INTEGRATION:-}, but changing gateLine breaks appendGate's strings.Contains idempotency check, so existing installs would get a duplicate gate. Requires appendGate to recognise+replace the old nocx block (migration), not just append. Deferred from nocx-zrd. PR #1 / nocx-5mn.4.

title: Make the main PR CI check a required check (branch protection), once the suite is reliably green
description: nocx-q36 restored ci.yml pull_request:[main] and it fires, but the check is not yet *required* (no branch protection on main) and cannot be green until the flaky suite is fixed. Enable branch protection on main requiring the CI checks once nocx-zaj (transport -race flake, e.g. TestWSServer_FairnessTwoFloodingOneQuiet) and nocx-epm (e2e paste/sleep flake) are resolved. Completes nocx-q36's 'a PR shows a required green check' acceptance.

title: defer/harden remote SFTP shell-integration install — unwired & untested
description: install_remote.go (~165 lines) + github.com/pkg/sftp ship but WithRemoteInstaller has zero call sites (unreachable from production) and no tests. Latent bugs: login-shell -l vs .bashrc gate mismatch (remote bash never sources); world-readable remote file perms (no chmod vs local 0600); appendGateRemote TOCTOU dup; GetRemoteHome parses echo $HOME (banner pollution); ctx ignored. Decision: strip from PR #1 or land explicitly staged, fix latent bugs before wiring under the SSH epic. PR #1 / nocx-5mn.4.

title: [zsh] exit-code capture fragile to pre-registered precmd hooks
description: nocx.zsh registers __nocx_capture_status as a separate precmd hook that must run first; oh-my-zsh/plugins registered earlier clobber $? before it. Capture $? as the first line of a single precmd instead. PR #1 / nocx-5mn.4.

title: shell integration writes VERSION before rc gate; failed gate append never retried
description: shellintegration.go EnsureInstalled writes VERSION (l.105) before the appendGate loop (l.109-117, non-fatal). Next run short-circuits on version match, so a transient rc-write failure leaves scripts installed but never sourced. Same in EnsureInstalledRemote. Fix: write VERSION last / track gate success. PR #1 / nocx-5mn.4.

title: [bash] duplicate OSC 133 C markers on pipelines/lists
description: DEBUG trap fires per simple command; 'ls | grep x' emits multiple 133;C markers. Needs a first-command-after-prompt latch (bash-preexec style). PR #1 / nocx-5mn.4.

title: shell integration scripts abort rc load under set -u
description: nocx.bash:9 / nocx.zsh:9 reference $__nocx_loaded (and :5 $NOCX_SHELL_INTEGRATION) without :- guard; under set -u / setopt nounset the first source hits unbound-variable and aborts the rest of the user's rc. Use ${__nocx_loaded:-}. PR #1 / nocx-5mn.4.

title: Containerize pre-commit test steps (go test + vitest in OrbStack)
description: The pre-commit hook ran go test -race and vitest on the host FS. Per the testing-isolation policy, tests must run in a container, never against the developer's real filesystem. Add .githooks/containerized-tests.sh (go_test_containerized, vitest_containerized) and swap the two test steps in .githooks/pre-commit to call them; linters/format/typecheck stay on host. Verified: both runners green in OrbStack (all Go packages incl. -race; 155 vitest tests).
close_reason: Implemented: .githooks/containerized-tests.sh + pre-commit swaps go test/vitest to OrbStack containers. Verified both runners green.

title: App-owned command history + basic completion
description: nocx owns history UI and basic completion (history, PATH executables, cwd-aware files/dirs). Native plugin-accurate shell completion is a later per-shell adapter or an explicit native-input escape hatch, not mirrored live.
acceptance_criteria: Up/down and Ctrl-R browse app history; Tab offers PATH + cwd path completion at PROMPT_READY.

title: OSC-133-driven block decorations (no freeze)
description: Create xterm markers at OSC 133 boundaries carrying metadata (command, cwd, start/end, exit code); draw lightweight decorations (separators, status glyph, exit-code colour, hover actions). Output stays in one continuous xterm; NO freeze-to-DOM. On alt-screen hide chrome + editor.
acceptance_criteria: Each command gets a boundary marker + exit-code-coloured indicator; selection/output fidelity remain xterm's; alt-screen hides chrome.

title: Frontend readiness signal: a tab reports whether it actually started
description:
TabManager's constructor calls newTab() without awaiting it (frontend/src/tabs.ts:443), and Tab.start() catches its own failure and renders a .pane-error notice into the pane (frontend/src/tabs.ts:356). So a constructed TabManager is fully compatible with a terminal that never started — there is currently no way for anything to ask 'did the terminal actually come up?'.

Work: Tab exposes whether its renderer mounted and its PTY session opened; TabManager exposes a promise resolving when the initial tab reaches that state. The existing swallow-and-show-a-notice behaviour stays for the UI — it simply stops counting as success.

Useful on its own (it is the honest startup signal the app lacks), and a hard prerequisite for the updater's health check: without it the updater would bless a release whose terminal failed to start, then delete the rollback copy. Found by adversarial review of the distribution design, docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-22-distribution-and-updates-design.md (S7.5).
acceptance_criteria: A test proves the promise resolves for a genuinely started tab and does not resolve for one whose start() threw.

title: Payload round trip and manifest verification, proven before anything swaps a bundle
description:
The two mechanisms the whole updater rests on, landed and proven while a failure is still harmless. Design: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-22-distribution-and-updates-design.md (S9).

1. Payload round trip: a genuinely built .app through ditto -c -k then ditto -x -k, asserting the executable bit, symlink targets, lipo -archs still showing both slices, codesign --verify --deep --strict still passing (Wails applies an ad-hoc signature and packaging must not damage it), and that the extracted bundle launches. This is a macOS integration test, not a unit test with a fixture. The first design draft planned to extract with Go's archive/zip, which restores nothing on its own and would have produced regular non-executable files with no symlinks — this test is what makes that class of error impossible to ship.

2. Manifest verification against the ed25519 keyring: valid, tampered body, key outside the keyring, valid signature from a second key in the keyring, malformed base64. Fixture keys generated in the test, no network.
acceptance_criteria: Both tests pass against a real CI-produced artefact, and the round trip fails if the archive is created with plain zip instead of ditto.

title: Go tests flake under load: session Done channel and ws size contract time out
description:
Two Go tests fail intermittently and pass in isolation, both by timing out rather than by asserting anything false:
- internal/session TestRealRegistry_DoneChannel — 'Done channel never closed' after 5.10s
- internal/transport TestWSServer_SizeContract_OpenAtSize — 'read: i/o timeout' after 5.00s

Caught by the pre-commit hook on a commit that changed only .beads/*.jsonl — no Go code at all — and the same tests had passed on the preceding commit minutes earlier. Re-run individually: both ok. Full 'go test -race ./...' afterwards: all green. So the failure is load-dependent, not a regression.

Both use fixed deadlines around real goroutine and socket work, which is the same class as the e2e flake in nocx-epm: a duration that holds on an idle machine and loses when the box is busy. -race makes it worse by slowing everything it instruments.

Worth fixing rather than tolerating — a hook that fails for unrelated reasons trains people to re-run until green, and the next real failure gets re-run too. Look at whether the deadlines can be replaced by waiting on the actual signal, and whether these two can stop competing for the machine with the rest of the suite.
acceptance_criteria: go test -race ./... passes twenty consecutive runs on a loaded machine, and neither test uses a fixed deadline as its synchronisation.

title: Auto-update mechanism
description:
Keep an installed nocx current without a manual re-download. Design: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-22-distribution-and-updates-design.md (S7).

The design survived three adversarial review rounds; round 3 broke an earlier phase-machine design in five places and it was replaced rather than patched. The load-bearing idea: THE FILESYSTEM IS THE TRUTH, THE JOURNAL RECORDS INTENT. The record holds device+inode of the old and new bundles; reconciliation observes which identity sits at which path and derives state from that. There is no prepared/swapped/healthy phase to go stale between a mutation and the write describing it. Versions cannot do this job (a same-version re-release, a reinstall and a downgrade share version strings), and string path comparison cannot either (symlinks and the /Applications vs /System/Volumes/Data/Applications alias).

Work: internal/update with Check/Apply/Reconcile/ReportHealthy; ed25519 keyring verification of the manifest; archive preflight via archive/zip BEFORE invoking ditto; extraction via /usr/bin/ditto -x -k --noqtn (archive/zip cannot restore a bundle); atomic exchange via unix.RenameatxNp with RENAME_SWAP; advisory flock on an open descriptor (never O_EXCL, which survives SIGKILL); bounded try-lock at startup so the terminal never waits on someone else's download; auto-rollback after three launches that reach Go without confirming health; legible refusals; UI notice and README.

Depends on the payload round-trip proof and the frontend readiness signal — both are prerequisites, not part of this bead.
acceptance_criteria: A running nocx detects a newer published release and can move the user to it, and the update it installs launches.

title: macOS code signing and notarization for distributable builds
description:
An unsigned .app downloaded from the internet is refused by Gatekeeper — the user gets 'damaged and cannot be opened', not a warning they can click through. Every route to another person's Mac needs this, and an updater needs it twice over: it replaces the running application, and an unsigned replacement will not launch.

Needs an Apple Developer account (paid, annual), a Developer ID Application certificate exported into CI secrets, codesign with hardened runtime, and notarytool submission plus stapling. Wails supports signing in wails.json but the notarization step is ours to script.

Cost and ownership are the owner's call, not a technical detail to assume: this is the first item in the project that requires a paid account and long-lived secrets in the repository.

Linux has no equivalent gate; AppImage and .deb run unsigned. This bead is macOS-only by nature.
acceptance_criteria: A CI-produced macOS build downloaded on another Mac opens without a Gatekeeper refusal.
close_reason: Out of scope per D3 of docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-22-distribution-and-updates-design.md. There is no Apple Developer ID and there will not be one, so this cannot be done: notarization requires Developer ID, which requires paid enrolment; no third-party service signs in its own name (SignPath stores YOUR certificate, it does not issue one); and Homebrew closed the workaround — --no-quarantine is removed from brew and from 2026-09-01 every cask must pass Gatekeeper. An earlier design kept a dormant 'sign if secrets exist' branch in the pipeline; review round 1 correctly called it speculative and a half-truth, since real Developer ID distribution needs signing order, hardened runtime, entitlements, notarytool and stapling rather than a secrets toggle. What IS in scope and stays: Wails' unconditional ad-hoc signature, which packaging must preserve and verify. Reopen only if a team ID ever becomes available.

title: Release builds for macOS in CI
description:
Produce an installable macOS artefact from CI and publish it. Design: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-22-distribution-and-updates-design.md (S4, S5, S6).

SCOPE CORRECTION (2026-07-23, after three review rounds): macOS only. Linux is deferred (D1) because a Wails Linux build is dynamically linked against GTK/WebKitGTK/libsoup/glibc, so an Ubuntu-built artefact is not a portable 'linux amd64' artefact, and there is no identified Linux user. docs/architecture.md:179 therefore stays as written and is NOT amended.

Work:
- internal/version with Version/Commit/Date set via -ldflags (Wails v2.13 passes them to both universal slices).
- Patch wails.json info.productVersion from the tag: the object does not exist today, so 1.0.0 is Wails' default and the plist would otherwise disagree with the linked Go version. Releases restricted to stable vMAJOR.MINOR.PATCH because CFBundleVersion has a narrower grammar than semver.
- ci.yml gains workflow_call and LOSES its push tags trigger (otherwise every release runs the suite twice).
- release.yml on tags, calls ci.yml so a release cannot exist without green backend/frontend/e2e on that commit. workflow_dispatch is dry-run only: a manual run has no tag and cannot produce the release URLs the manifest asserts.
- Build on macos-15 (explicit image), verify with lipo -archs and codesign --verify --deep --strict, package .dmg via hdiutil and .zip via ditto -c -k --keepParent --noqtn.
- Publish job (separate, so the signing key never sits in a job running build tooling) generates manifest.json, signs it with ed25519, attaches both to the release.

Ends with a downloadable build installed BY HAND on another Mac. No updater code here.
acceptance_criteria: A tagged commit produces downloadable macOS and Linux artefacts from CI, and each one launches on a clean machine of its platform.

title: [epic] Epic: Distribution and updates
description:
Getting nocx onto other people's machines and keeping it current. Design (three adversarial review rounds): docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-22-distribution-and-updates-design.md

The two doc decisions this epic said had to be revisited before work started are now RESOLVED:
- docs/architecture.md:179 ('MVP is macOS-only; Windows/Linux are Phase 3') — STAYS AS WRITTEN. Linux is deferred (nocx-mbu): a Wails Linux build is dynamically linked against GTK/WebKitGTK/libsoup/glibc, so an Ubuntu-built artefact is not portable, and there is no Linux user today.
- docs/vision.md:89 ('no app-store or packaged distribution') — IS AMENDED, inside nocx-a75.1/nocx-a75.3, to describe GitHub Releases, unsigned builds, a self-hosted update chain, and the manual quarantine step.

The constraint that shaped everything: there is no Apple Developer ID and there will not be one. Signing and notarization are therefore out of scope (nocx-a75.2, closed), the first install needs a manual xattr, and update integrity is ours — an ed25519-signed manifest verified against a compiled-in keyring. Homebrew is not an escape: --no-quarantine is gone from brew and every cask must pass Gatekeeper from 2026-09-01.

Order matters and is deliberate: an updater defect can remove the application itself, so the artefact path ships and gets used by hand (a75.1) and its two load-bearing mechanisms are proven (nocx-3dk) before anything replaces a bundle automatically (a75.3).

title: Settings registry: declare user-facing settings in one place so the settings screen is generated, not remembered
description:
Problem this solves is process, not code: deferred settings get forgotten. The terminal clipboard work left two user decisions living as ad-hoc state — the OSC 52 write grant (ClipboardGate) and copy-on-select (shouldCopy, currently unconditional) — with nothing but a bead to remind anyone to surface them when the settings screen arrives. This backlog has already demonstrated that reminders drift: three items finished in code sat open in the tracker and were offered as next work.

Invert it. Declare each user-facing setting once — id, label, description, default, current value, and whether it persists — and have the settings screen RENDER THAT LIST rather than being hand-written. Then surfacing a setting is not something to remember; forgetting requires deleting an entry, which is a deliberate act rather than an omission.

Not speculative: five consumers are already filed — nocx-8yg.5 (font family/size), nocx-8yg.6 (colour schemes), nocx-8yg.7 (keybindings), plus the two clipboard decisions above. All four settings-facing beads are blocked by this one, so none of them can be picked up without going through the registry.

Frontend-only and buildable now; it does not wait on storage. nocx-jap (persistent config) then binds the registry to a backing store, and entries flip from ephemeral to persisted in one place. Name matches the domain language already in use — internal/config and 'settings', not a third word for the same thing.
acceptance_criteria: Every user-facing preference is declared in one module; ClipboardGate and copy-on-select read their values from it; adding a preference requires no edit to a settings screen.

title: e2e activity-bell is flaky under parallel load (fixed sleeps race the shell)
description:
e2e/activity-bell.spec.ts fails intermittently when the whole suite runs, and passes 5/5 when run alone. Observed on webkit: 'expect(locator).toBeAttached() failed' on the activity indicator. Pre-existing — the spec landed in ffb36b7, an ancestor of 1537d18, and the terminal-clipboard work did not touch it. Surfaced incidentally while verifying that change.

Cause is time-based synchronisation. The test types a command that enters the alternate screen, sleeps 5s in the SHELL, rings the bell, then sleeps 30s. It then waits a fixed 2000ms before backgrounding the tab and a fixed 6000ms before asserting. Five spec files share one wails dev instance across parallel workers, so under load the shell can take long enough to start that the bell lands after the 6s window closes and the assertion runs before the indicator lights.

Fix the same way e2e/clipboard.spec.ts was fixed: replace fixed sleeps with state-based synchronisation — wait for an observable signal that the shell has actually reached each stage rather than guessing at durations. Do NOT raise the timeouts; that hides the race and it returns on a slower machine or in CI.
acceptance_criteria: The full suite passes ten consecutive runs, and the spec contains no fixed sleep used as synchronisation.

title: Persistent config: internal/config is an in-memory stub, so no user decision survives a restart
description:
internal/config/config.go is a 47-line Stub: a map in memory, with Load() and Save() as logged no-ops. Nothing a user chooses is remembered across restarts.

This became load-bearing with the OSC 52 clipboard gate: writes are denied by default and the banner offers 'Allow clipboard writes', but with no storage that grant lasts only until the app is closed, and 'Don't show again' cannot mean what it says. The same gap blocks every other setting the terminal will want — font, theme, keybindings, copy-on-select toggle.

Needs a real implementation behind the existing Config interface (Load/Save/Get/Set), a file location that fits macOS conventions, and a bound method so the frontend can read and write settings. Keep it behind the interface per AD-8; the Stub stays as the test double.
acceptance_criteria: A user decision written through Config survives an app restart, and the OSC 52 grant plus 'Don't show again' both persist.

title: OSC 52 grant: bind the user's decision to the program, not the whole app
description:
Today an OSC 52 clipboard-write grant is all-or-nothing for the app (Warp's model). Least privilege says the grant should name the program: allowing Claude Code to write the clipboard is far narrower than allowing anything running in any tab.

Identity MUST come from the kernel, never from anything the program controls. The obvious cheap route — matching the terminal title — is worthless: the title is set by the program itself (OSC 0/2), so a hostile program writes 'Claude Code' and inherits the grant. Use the PTY's foreground process group (TIOCGPGRP on the pty fd) and resolve it to an executable path. internal/pty/pty_local.go already holds cmd *exec.Cmd, so the fd and the child are in reach. Match on the full path, not the basename — 'claude' can be any file on PATH.

Where this degrades, and it is exactly where the risk is highest: over SSH there is no local process for the remote program, so the foreground process is 'ssh' and the grant collapses to 'any host you connect to'. Same inside tmux, where it collapses to 'tmux'. The SSH case likely needs a grant keyed by host rather than by process — decide that when SSH lands (nocx-9le).

Needs: a foreground-process lookup in the backend, a control-plane message to carry the identity to the frontend (AD-1/AD-6), and per-program grant state in the frontend policy. Depends on persistent config (the grant must outlive a restart to be worth having).
acceptance_criteria: A clipboard-write grant applies to one program identified from the kernel, not to the whole application, and a differently-named program does not inherit it.

title: Wire ValidateCwd into the OSC 7 path once SSH lands
description: internal/shellintegration.ValidateCwd is implemented and unit-tested but deliberately not called: nocx-5mn.2 parses OSC 7 in the frontend per AD-6 and uses the path directly. Every session today is local, so the host arrives empty and validating it would cost an IPC round trip per cd to confirm an empty string. AD-5 assigns the backend ownership of local-vs-remote and host validation, and that ownership only starts paying when the host comes from a remote shell. Decision taken with the repo owner, recorded in the interface doc comment so the code is not deleted as dead by a future reader.
acceptance_criteria: The OSC 7 handler routes host+path through ValidateCwd, a hostile or unexpected host is rejected rather than displayed, and a test covers the remote case.

title: shellintegration.ParseCwd contradicts AD-6 (backend must not parse OSC)
description: internal/shellintegration/shellintegration.go declares ParseCwd(data []byte) and ParsePromptMarker(data []byte) — a backend parser fed raw terminal bytes — and internal/app/app.go constructs it into the composition root. AD-6 is explicit that the VT frontend parses OSC 7/133 via parser.registerOscHandler and that 'the backend does not sniff the byte stream'; the conditional dependency was DISCHARGED by ADR-0001 with 'the backend never parses OSC'. AD-5 still leaves the backend a real job — it owns local-vs-remote and validates the host, while 'the frontend only supplies the desired path' — so the interface should take a path, not a byte stream. Nothing calls ParseCwd today, so this is drift in the shape of the seam rather than a live violation, but implementing nocx-5mn.2 against the current signature would cement it.
acceptance_criteria: The shell-integration seam takes a frontend-supplied path and returns validated {host,path}; no backend API accepts raw terminal bytes; AD-6 and the code agree.
close_reason:
Interface reshaped to AD-6: ValidateCwd(host, path) replaces ParseCwd(data []byte), so no backend API takes raw terminal bytes; ParsePromptMarker went with it rather than being kept speculatively. internal/app now holds the interface instead of the concrete Stub, matching the repo's interface-first/DI rule. Covered by internal/shellintegration tests.
Deliberately NOT wired into the OSC 7 path — decided with the repo owner and recorded both in the interface doc comment and in nocx-3p1. Every session is local today, so the host arrives empty and validating it would buy an IPC round trip per cd to confirm an empty string. The comment says plainly that it is unwired by choice, so it is not deleted as dead code.

title: [bug] Tab title disappears while the mouse is over the tab
description: Hovering a tab hides its title entirely. The rule is frontend/src/style.css '.tab:hover .tab-title', which applies a two-layer -webkit-mask-image intended only to fade the title under the 22px close button. The two layers combine to mask the whole label instead. Note WKWebView is the target engine, so verify the fix in the real app, not only in Chromium.
acceptance_criteria: Hovering a tab leaves the title readable, with at most a fade where the close button overlaps it.
close_reason: Verified by the repo owner in the real WKWebView app: the title stays at full strength and fades only under the close button. Fixed by dropping -webkit-mask entirely in favour of an absolutely-positioned ::after gradient overlay (34px, right edge, fading to the tab's current background via a --tab-bg custom property, z-index 1 under the close button's 2). Masks had failed twice here — first two layers compositing unpredictably, then a single gradient dimming the whole label — because WKWebView's defaults for mask-size/mask-repeat diverge from Chromium's. An ordinary positioned gradient has no engine-dependent behaviour.

title: [bug] New tab flashes 'Terminal' before showing the directory
description: Opening a tab shows 'Terminal' for a moment, then it becomes the directory name. Cause is visible in frontend/src/tabs.ts: _defaultTitle is initialised to FALLBACK_TITLE ('Terminal', line 17/51) and written into the DOM in the constructor (line 87), while the real name only arrives after 'await this.client.openSession(...)' resolves (lines 183-188). The tab is therefore painted with a placeholder every time.
acceptance_criteria: A newly opened tab never displays 'Terminal'; it shows the directory from its first painted frame.
close_reason: The constructor no longer paints FALLBACK_TITLE into the DOM; the label stays empty until openSession resolves with the directory name. Guarded by e2e/tab-title.spec.ts, which snapshots the label via a MutationObserver at the instant the tab node is inserted — before the fix that snapshot read 'Terminal'. Passes in both Playwright engines.

title: Closing a tab with a running program asks for confirmation
description: Closing a tab kills its session immediately. Other terminals ask first when something is still running, because closing a tab in the middle of a Claude Code session or a build loses work. Needs a way to tell 'a program is running' from 'an idle shell' — the foreground process differing from the shell, or OSC 133 command markers (nocx-5mn) — and a confirmation that is skippable for an idle shell so the common case stays one click.
design:
How Tabby does it (tabby-local/src/components/terminalTab.component.ts, canClose): it asks the PTY for the shell's child processes and, if there are any, shows '"{command}" is still running. Close?' with Kill / Cancel. No shell integration, no OSC 133 — just the process tree.

We can be more precise than a child list. The PTY exposes the terminal's FOREGROUND PROCESS GROUP via tcgetpgrp(fd): when it differs from the shell's own pid, a command is running in the foreground right now. That is exactly the moment to catch, and it is better than enumerating children because a backgrounded job ('sleep 100 &') is a child but must not block closing, while tcgetpgrp correctly ignores it. It also names the command: look up the pgid to report 'claude is still running'.

Placement: the backend owns the PTY, so this is a session query, not something the frontend can compute — and it must not be inferred from the byte stream (AD-6). Add a control-plane request, e.g. a JSON-RPC 'busy' method taking a sessionId and answering {busy, command}. The frontend asks before closing and only prompts when busy, so an idle shell still closes in one click.

Note the answer is a snapshot and can go stale between the query and the confirmation — that is acceptable here; the cost of a rare wrong prompt is far below the cost of losing a running session.
acceptance_criteria: Closing a tab running a program asks first; closing an idle shell does not.

title: New tab is named Terminal; name it after the working directory
description: Every comparable terminal labels a tab with its folder rather than a generic word — Warp shows '..cuments/repos/meshynet'. Our fallback is the literal 'Terminal', which tells the user nothing and makes several tabs indistinguishable until a program sets a title. Use the session's working directory as the fallback, shortened from the left when it does not fit, and keep letting OSC 0/2 override it while a program is running. OSC 7 already carries cwd changes (nocx-5mn.2) — the two should share one source of truth rather than each inventing a title.
acceptance_criteria: A new tab shows its working directory instead of 'Terminal', a program's title still wins while it runs, and the directory returns when that program exits.
close_reason: Closed

title: Right-click in the terminal opens the webview context menu (Reload / Inspect Element)
description: Right-clicking anywhere in the app shows WebKit's own menu with Reload and Inspect Element — developer surface leaking into a shipped terminal. Wails exposes EnableDefaultContextMenu in the app options; it should be off outside development. Right-click is also wanted later for paste (nocx-8yg.4), so whatever suppresses the default menu must leave that path open.
acceptance_criteria: Right-click in the terminal does not show Reload / Inspect Element in a non-dev build.
notes:
NOT fixed — reconfirmed by the owner with screenshots. EnableDefaultContextMenu:false is set (main.go:38) but is necessary, not sufficient, for two reasons found in the wails v2.13.0 source:
1. internal/frontend/desktop/darwin/window.go:62 — defaultContextMenuEnabled := bool2Cint(debug || frontendOptions.EnableDefaultContextMenu). In dev the native menu is ALWAYS on, so 'Reload / Inspect Element' under wails dev says nothing about the shipped build.
2. internal/frontend/runtime/desktop/contextmenu.js — with the default '--default-contextmenu: auto' the native menu still shows when the target is an input/textarea, contentEditable, or text is selected. xterm keeps focus in a helper textarea, so the Cut/Copy/Paste/Spelling menu reproduces in a production build too.
Fix therefore needs '--default-contextmenu: hide' (inherited, like --wails-draggable) plus our own menu. The owner wants the menu to be ours, which overlaps nocx-8yg.4 (copy-on-select + right-click paste).
close_reason: Right-click no longer raises the WebKit menu. EnableDefaultContextMenu:false was necessary but not sufficient, as the bead's own notes recorded: the fix is --default-contextmenu:hide on the pane, inherited like --wails-draggable. Right-click now pastes instead. Note the comment in style.css was corrected during review — a later listener cannot un-cancel an already-cancelled default, so the CSS is defence in depth rather than the mechanism the original note assumed.

title: Hovering a tab hides its label
description: The mask-image gradient copied from Tabby's tabHeader.component.scss fades the title under the action buttons on hover. Tabby sizes it for its own button cluster (mask-size: calc(100% - 80px) auto, 80px auto); with our single close button the 80px fade swallows most of a 200px tab, so the label disappears instead of fading under a button. Size the mask to the buttons we actually render, or drop the mask and let text-overflow: ellipsis do the work.
acceptance_criteria: Hovering a tab reveals the close button without making the label unreadable.
close_reason: Closed

title: Layout tests in a real browser (vitest browser mode)
description: jsdom has no layout engine: offsetWidth/getBoundingClientRect return 0 and flexbox is never computed, so no jsdom test can catch a geometry regression. Two such bugs already shipped past green suites and were only caught by looking: a lone tab stretching to the full window, then the tab collapsing to 107px instead of 200px. The route is wails dev, which serves the whole app (bound Go methods included) on http://localhost:34115 — the documented way to drive a Wails app from a browser. Run Playwright against that: wails dev in one terminal, playwright test in another. Cover layout invariants only, keeping the fast jsdom suite for behaviour: a lone tab must not span the bar, a tab must hold its 200px width rather than collapsing to its label, the + button sits next to the last tab rather than at the window edge, tabs shrink rather than overflow as their count grows, and the active chip is distinguishable from the bar.
acceptance_criteria: A browser-mode test suite runs in CI and the hook, and a deliberate flex-grow regression on .tab fails it.

title: Dev seam: fixed WS port so the frontend runs in a plain browser
description: frontend/src/main.ts falls back to port 9876 when the Wails runtime is absent (plain browser), but internal/transport/ws.go listens on 127.0.0.1:0 — a random port — so the fallback can never connect. The escape hatch is dead code today. Make the port injectable (e.g. NOCX_WS_PORT env var, default 0 = random as now) so 'npm run dev' plus a backend on a known port gives a working terminal in Chrome. Unlocks browser devtools for frontend iteration and is the precondition for screenshotting/【layout-testing】the UI without building the Wails app.
acceptance_criteria: With NOCX_WS_PORT set, the Vite dev server in a plain browser opens a working session; with it unset the port stays random as today.
close_reason: Not needed — wails dev already serves the whole app, bound Go methods included, at http://localhost:34115. The Vite-only port (5173) has no backend by definition; that was my mistake, not a missing seam. Browser-based UI checking and e2e go through 34115.

title: Restore CI on PR to main once the MVP has something to protect
description: CI triggers were deliberately narrowed to release/**, v* tags and workflow_dispatch (commit 0adab78). Rationale from the repo owner: nothing works end-to-end yet, so spending macOS runner minutes on every push and PR buys nothing. Consequence to accept meanwhile: nothing mechanically enforces the quality gates on main — the pre-commit hook is bypassable with --no-verify and 'make hooks' is a manual step a fresh clone may skip. Restore 'pull_request: branches: [main]' (keeping the release-branch and tag triggers) once the MVP is usable end-to-end, and re-assert 'no merge without green' in docs/architecture.md at the same time.
acceptance_criteria: ci.yml triggers on pull_request to main again; docs/architecture.md and README agree; a PR shows a required green check.

title: core.hooksPath=.githooks shadows the beads git hooks
description: make hooks sets core.hooksPath=.githooks. Beads installs its own five hooks (pre-commit, post-merge, pre-push, post-checkout, prepare-commit-msg) as thin shims calling 'bd hooks run <name>'. They were never installed in this clone (.git/hooks/ is empty and no commit in history carries a beads trailer), so nothing was broken — but the two systems now collide: 'bd hooks list' already misreports 'pre-commit: installed (version )' with an empty version because it finds .githooks/pre-commit without the beads marker, and a future 'bd hooks install' would be inert or would clobber our gate. Decide whether we want the beads hooks at all; if yes, chain them from .githooks/ so both run.
design:
Chain beads from .githooks/, keep one hook root. Decided with the owner after a design review by codex; both of its corrections were verified independently.

Cadence: on git push, not per-commit or per-bd-mutation.

1. .beads/config.yaml — export.auto: true, path: issues.jsonl, git-add: true. Tracked, so contributors inherit it with no command of their own.
2. .githooks/pre-commit — beads section AFTER the quality gates, so a failed 30s gate never leaves issues.jsonl staged. Needed despite export.auto because there is no daemon in bd 1.0.5: auto-export runs after write commands throttled to 60s, so a last mutation inside the throttle window would miss the commit.
3. .githooks/pre-push — new shim running bd dolt push via 'bd hooks run pre-push'.
4. Shared .githooks/beads-hook.sh so both hooks carry one copy of the policy.

Call 'bd hooks run <name>', never a hand-rolled 'bd dolt push': the shim keeps behaviour tied to the installed CLI. Keep BD_GIT_HOOK=1; bd 1.0.5 also suppresses hooks internally during its own git operations, so recursion is covered from both ends.

Failure policy is HYBRID, and deliberately unlike the stock beads shim, which ends in 'exit $_bd_exit' and so blocks git on any error:
  - bd absent, or exit 3 (no database): skip. A contributor who does not use beads must still commit and push.
  - genuine sync failure with bd present: block, and name the fix. That is the case that silently leaves the remote stale.
Must not call bd bare under 'set -eu' — capture the status via '|| bd_exit=$?' or the hook dies before the policy runs.

Known limitation, to be documented rather than hidden: Dolt-canonical + non-blocking + always-fresh clones cannot all hold at once. bd bootstrap prefers sync.remote over the tracked JSONL (JSONL is fourth in its precedence list), so a push that never happened still yields a stale clone.

Onboarding changes to 'bd bootstrap', not 'bd init'; 'bd init --from-jsonl' is recovery only.
acceptance_criteria: Either beads hooks are deliberately declined and that is documented, or .githooks/ chains 'bd hooks run <name>' for each hook and 'bd hooks list' reports accurately.
close_reason:
Beads now syncs from .githooks/: pre-commit exports and stages .beads/issues.jsonl, pre-push runs bd dolt push. One hook root, both files tracked, wired by the existing 'make hooks'.

Two design assumptions were wrong and were corrected by measurement, not argument:
1. 'bd hooks run pre-commit' does NOT force a JSONL export here — it exits 0 and changes nothing. pre-commit calls 'bd export -o' directly instead, which is exact and synchronous. export.auto: true is still enabled, but only to keep the file warm between commits: it is throttled to 60s and the write does not reliably land before the command returns.
2. 'bd hooks run pre-push' is a silent no-op on bd 1.0.5 — exit 0 with refs/dolt/data unmoved. 'bd dolt push' moves it. The hook calls that. Worth re-testing on the next bd upgrade, since the shim is the documented interface (Homebrew already offers 1.1.0 against the 1.0.5 installed here).

Failure policy is hybrid and was tested against all three branches with a stub bd: missing bd -> skip (0), exit 3 no database -> skip (0), genuine failure -> block with the fix spelled out. This differs from the stock beads shim, which ends in 'exit $_bd_exit' and blocks git on any error.

Verified end to end: a fresh clone plus 'bd bootstrap' now reports nocx-d1f CLOSED, where before this change the same clone reported it as an open P0 while the issues.jsonl beside it said closed.

title: [bake-off] wterm translates wheel to arrow keys — scrolls prompt history, not the screen
description: In the wterm tab the wheel pages through hermes' message history instead of scrolling the viewport. Captures confirm hermes never enables mouse tracking (modes 1000/1002/1003/1006 appear only as disables at teardown, never as enables), so the wheel is the renderer's own to interpret. wterm appears to translate it into Up/Down key presses, which prompt_toolkit reads as history navigation. xterm.js scrolls its own scrollback as expected. Renderer-specific — a bake-off differentiator against wterm.

title: M0 terminal clipped at top: first row(s) partially hidden (layout/CSS)
description: The terminal content is slightly cut off at the top - the first row is partially hidden. Frontend layout/CSS: #terminal container or ghostty-web canvas positioning clips the top.
acceptance_criteria: Top row fully visible, nothing clipped; canvas fits within the viewport container.
close_reason: Closed

title: Console flood: ghostty-web logs a warning per unrecognized OSC (OSC 697 Fig/zsh each prompt)
description: ghostty-web console.log('[ghostty-vt] warning(osc): invalid OSC command: ...') for every OSC it does not handle. User's zsh Fig/Amazon-Q integration emits many OSC 697 per prompt -> hundreds of console logs; apps also emit OSC 10/11/12/99/1337. Cosmetic (terminal works) but floods the inspector.
acceptance_criteria: Unrecognized-OSC warnings no longer flood the console during normal shell use (via ghostty-web log level if available, else a scoped console filter).
close_reason: Closed

title: Copy-on-mouse-select + right-click paste
description: Selection + clipboard.
close_reason: Copy-on-select and paste on both the right and middle buttons, matching Warp and Tabby defaults. Selection reaches the clipboard through a policy layer above the renderer (AD-6); clipboard access is constructed in main.ts and injected (AD-8). rightClickSelectsWord:false is load-bearing — xterm defaults it true on macOS, so right-click word-selected, copy-on-select overwrote the clipboard, and the paste delivered the word under the pointer. Multi-line paste is confirmed outside the alternate screen (Tabby's condition). 155 unit tests, e2e in Chromium (WebKit skipped: Playwright has no clipboard permissions there), every test proven to fail when its own feature is sabotaged.

title: Restore tabs on restart
description: Restore record = backend {sessionId,kind,host} + FE cwd snapshot; config persists.
notes: Reference design: zellij's session persistence (zellij-utils/src/session_serialization.rs + zellij-server/src/session_layout_metadata.rs) records per-pane cwd plus the running command and replays it to rebuild the workspace. Pairs with the OSC 7 cwd events in nocx-5mn.2. We do not embed zellij — see ADR-0002 (docs/decisions/0002-native-tabs-no-embedded-multiplexer.md).

title: OSC 7 cwd events {host,path}; copy-folder-path action
description: AD-5/AD-6. Percent-decoded {host,path}; backend owns local-vs-remote; $HOME fallback surfaced.
close_reason:
OSC 7 is parsed frontend-side via parser.registerOscHandler(7, ...) per AD-6, percent-decoded into {host, path}, with malformed payloads rejected rather than displayed. The tab title and tooltip now follow cd, and a title set by a running program is no longer clobbered by a directory change (a separate _programTitle tracks it). The session-open cwd is surfaced as '(initial cwd)' in the tooltip rather than passed off as a live reading, which is AD-5's requirement that the fallback be visible.
Covered by 110 frontend tests including new xterm.test.ts cases for the parser. Copy-folder-path was split out to nocx-203; it needs UI wiring (clipboard + shortcut) that belongs with the context menu work.

title: PTY-based regression test for OSC 133 C-marker latch
description: nocx-bvo (dup C markers) is fixed via a per-line latch but only verified by inspection — the DEBUG-trap path needs an interactive shell to exercise. Add a creack/pty-driven test that runs 'ls | grep x' in a real interactive bash and asserts exactly one 133;C per entered line. PR #1 / nocx-5mn.4.

title: [bash] array-form PROMPT_COMMAND (bash 5.1+) loses elements beyond [0]
description: nocx.bash captures __nocx_old_pc="$PROMPT_COMMAND" as a scalar; on bash>=5.1 where PROMPT_COMMAND can be an array, only element 0 is preserved and the user's other prompt commands are dropped. Handle the array form. Review finding #7, PR #1 / nocx-5mn.4.

title: Agent mode: AgentInputTarget on the same editor
description: Second InputTarget routing submit to the LLM controller on the SAME editor, with separate draft + history and an explicit mode indicator. Depends on the InputTarget abstraction and the editor surface.
acceptance_criteria: Switching to agent mode routes submit to the LLM path; drafts/histories are per-mode; editor layout/selection/completion UI are shared.

title: Copy folder path action (from OSC 7 cwd)
description: Deferred out of nocx-5mn.2, whose bead mentioned it: the cwd is now tracked live via OSC 7, but there is no way to copy it. Needs its own UI wiring — clipboard API plus a keyboard shortcut and/or an entry in the context menu being designed in nocx-b05.
acceptance_criteria: The current folder path can be copied to the clipboard from the tab.

title: [bug] Text squeezes for a frame when a window resize starts
description: Reported by the owner while verifying nocx-q18: dragging the window edge briefly squashes the already-rendered text before it settles. Distinct from the glyph corruption that was just fixed — nothing is mangled, the frame is simply wrong. Likely mechanism: the WebGL canvas is stretched to the new window size immediately, while the grid is only recomputed after the ResizeObserver debounce (RESIZE_MIN_INTERVAL = 32ms) plus a rAF in frontend/src/renderers/xterm.ts, so at least one frame shows the old glyphs scaled to the new box. Worth checking how Tabby/VS Code avoid it — options include not scaling the canvas until the fit lands, or repainting synchronously on the first resize event rather than after the debounce.
acceptance_criteria: Dragging a window edge never shows a squashed or stretched frame of the previous content.

title: Root-level TypeScript is outside every format and lint gate
description: prettier and eslint only run inside frontend/ (frontend/package.json scripts, and the prettier config lives at frontend/.prettierrc.json), so playwright.config.ts and everything in e2e/ is checked by nothing. Confirmed: with default settings prettier rejects all four root TS files, including e2e/activity.spec.ts and e2e/seed.spec.ts written before this was noticed; run with --config frontend/.prettierrc.json those last two still fail. Files there are kept in the project style only by hand. Either hoist the prettier/eslint config to the repo root and widen the scripts, or add a root-level format:check covering e2e/ and playwright.config.ts, then fix the two stragglers.
acceptance_criteria: A deliberate style violation in e2e/ or playwright.config.ts fails a gate, and every root TS file passes it.

title: Title bar lightens on hover
description: Hovering the 'nocx' title bar makes it visibly lighter. Reported by the user against the Wails build; not yet reproduced or diagnosed — could be a hover rule bleeding from the tab bar CSS, or a native title-bar effect. Reproduce first, then decide. Note nocx-cpp removes the separate title bar entirely (frameless, Tabby-style), which may make this moot — check that before spending effort here.
acceptance_criteria: Either the hover highlight is gone, or the bead is closed as superseded by nocx-cpp.
close_reason: Superseded by the frameless work in 2c02a46, which is the second branch of this bead's own acceptance criteria. The element that lightened was the native macOS title bar; mac.TitleBarHidden removes it entirely, so there is nothing left to highlight. No dedicated fix commit exists — checked the whole log, nothing references this bead. Confirmed in style.css: hover rules exist only on .tab, .tab-close and .tab-add; .tabbar itself has none.

title: Evaluate gremlins for Go mutation testing
description: Mutation testing measures whether tests actually assert on behaviour rather than just executing it. Candidate: github.com/go-gremlins/gremlins v0.6.0 (2025-12-05, actively maintained). Rejected alternatives: gtramontina/ooze last released 2023-02-13, zimmski/go-mutesting last commit 2021-06-10 — both stale. Constraint: this suite spawns real PTYs (transport 2.5s, session 1.8s, pty 1.5s per run) and mutation testing reruns the suite once per mutant, so it cannot live in the pre-commit hook — expose it as a separate make target run on demand. Scope the first run to internal/transport and internal/session. Note it would NOT have caught the two critical defects found in review (a never-resolving promise in untested frontend code, and a goroutine ordering race), but it would have flagged the untested session-exit path.
acceptance_criteria: A make target runs gremlins over internal/transport and internal/session; surviving mutants are triaged into beads or dismissed with a reason.

title: CI actions target Node.js 20, forced onto Node 24 by the runner
description: Run 29822238195 emitted deprecation annotations on both jobs: actions/checkout@v4, actions/setup-node@v4 and actions/setup-go@v5 target Node.js 20, which GitHub has deprecated and is force-running on Node.js 24. Warnings only today; they become hard failures once GitHub drops the Node 20 shim. Bump the actions to majors that target Node 24 and confirm a run stays green.
acceptance_criteria: A CI run completes with no Node.js 20 deprecation annotations.

title: pre-commit hook checks the worktree, not the staged snapshot
description: Known limitation of .githooks/pre-commit: it runs gofumpt/golangci-lint/go test/tsc against the working tree. With a partially staged commit (some edits staged, others not), unstaged changes can make the tree pass while the committed snapshot would fail CI. The pre-commit framework solves this by stashing unstaged changes; a hand-rolled stash is risky (can lose work if the hook is interrupted). Decide deliberately: accept and document the limitation (CI stays the authoritative gate), or implement a safe stash-and-restore.
acceptance_criteria: Either the limitation is documented in AGENTS.md as a deliberate tradeoff, or the hook validates the staged snapshot safely.

title: Missing glyphs: some TUI symbols (powerline/Nerd Font) render as '?' — ghostty-web font lacks them
description: Agent TUIs (e.g. Hermes) use special glyphs (powerline separators / Nerd Font icons) that ghostty-web's default font does not include, so they render as replacement '?'/box chars. Cosmetic, low priority. Fix later: bundle/configure a font with wide glyph coverage (a Nerd Font) or investigate ghostty-web font fallback.
close_reason: Not a font bug. The '?' glyphs were U+FFFD from UTF-8 sequences split across WebSocket frames (see the ipc.ts decode fix), not missing Nerd Font/powerline coverage. Glyph probe across all three renderers came back clean.

title: Linux distribution — reopen when there is a user and a support envelope
description:
Deferred deliberately, not forgotten. Design: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-22-distribution-and-updates-design.md (D1).

A Wails Linux build is dynamically linked against GTK, WebKitGTK, libsoup and glibc, so an artefact built on Ubuntu 24.04 runs on the distributions that happen to match and nowhere else; a tarball installs no dependencies, and a .desktop file inside it integrates with nothing. Shipping it would mean choosing and testing a support envelope for a platform with no identified user today.

Reopen when BOTH exist: a person who will actually run it, and a decision about which distributions are supported. At that point the realistic choice is AppImage (linuxdeploy plus its GTK plugin, which brings its own class of WebKitGTK-versus-host breakage) rather than a bare tarball. Note this also moves docs/architecture.md:179, which currently says Windows/Linux are Phase 3 and is accurate while this is deferred.

title: frontend/dist/index.html is tracked but its hashed assets are gitignored
description: git tracks frontend/dist/index.html while .gitignore excludes frontend/dist/* — so the repo carries an HTML file referencing asset filenames that are not in the repo, and it churns on every npm run build. Compounding it, the negation '!frontend/dist/index.html' sits ABOVE 'frontend/dist/*' in .gitignore, and since the last matching pattern wins, the negation is overridden and only has no effect because the file is already tracked. Pre-existing, not caused by any recent change. Decide: untrack dist entirely (Wails regenerates it), or fix the pattern order and commit the assets.
acceptance_criteria: Either frontend/dist is fully untracked, or the .gitignore pattern order is corrected and the tracked set is self-consistent.
